narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 40.5% chance that you would not pass—above the threshold we use for admissions decisions. This prediction draws from your academic record, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.60, LSAT score of 37.50, Year 1 and Year 3 decile rankings both at 2, middle family income bracket, and full-time enrollment plans. Among students who passed the bar, the average undergraduate GPA is 3.24, and decile rankings average around 6 in Year 1 and 5.9 in Year 3, showing your grades placed you lower in your class compared to those who succeeded. The three most influential factors in this decision were your LSAT score, Year 3 decile ranking, and Year 1 decile ranking. Your solid LSAT performance was the strongest positive signal, pushing the prediction toward expecting you to pass by standing out favorably against typical passers. In contrast, your Year 3 decile ranking had the biggest negative pull, signaling to the model a heightened risk of bar failure because it was notably below what successful students typically achieve. Your Year 1 decile ranking added further concern in the same way, reinforcing the model's worry about early academic struggles carrying over. While your middle income and full-time status align reasonably with passers—where full-time is the norm at 93% and middle income is common—they weren't decisive here. This combination left the model leaning toward caution, prioritizing bar passage as a key indicator of long-term success in law. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to strengthen that asset or focusing on boosting your decile rankings through targeted academic support, perhaps via tutoring or additional coursework. Reapplying after addressing these areas could shift the prediction in your favor, and many students in similar positions have succeeded by doing just that. You're clearly motivated, and with strategic improvements, law school remains within reach.
